name: Bent-axis layout
description: Floor-layout strategy in which the principal circulation axis from the front door to the inner destination room (great hall, throne room, main shrine, lord's chamber) is deliberately broken by one or two right-angle turns, forming an L or Z plan rather than a straight nave. A small transition room, a freestanding screen wall, or a threshold pavilion marks the bend so that an arriving visitor cannot see the inner sanctum from the entrance and must commit to the turn to reveal it. The detour stretches the perceived length of the procession, multiplies the number of controlled thresholds and removes the direct line of sight that an attacker or an uninvited guest could exploit. This pattern is the signature plan of Chinese siheyuan compounds, Japanese shoin temple precincts and many fortified medieval gatehouses.
placement_rules: The principal axis from the front door to the inner destination room must contain one or two 90-degree turns; a straight line of sight between the two endpoints is forbidden.
Place an opaque element at the first turn — a screen wall, a spirit wall, a small antechamber or a threshold pavilion — so the inner room is invisible from the entrance even when every door is open.
Each leg of the bent axis is at least 3 blocks long, so the turn reads as a deliberate procession rather than an accidental jog in a corridor.
The leg landing at the inner destination is the longest segment, so the final room is revealed gradually as the visitor completes the last turn.
Doors along the bent axis align with the centre of each leg; secondary rooms hang off the sides of the axis, never break through it.
The outer perimeter wall remains closed on the side that would otherwise allow a direct view from the street to the inner room — no windows or openings shortcut the bent path.
Reserve one block of clearance on each side of the axis legs to form a continuous corridor of at least 3 blocks total width where the procession passes.

chinese.notes: Siheyuan plan with a yingbi spirit-screen wall of red terracotta facing the front gate, forcing arrivals to step around it; the bent axis then threads through successive courtyards on a stone-brick paving, each separated by a timber-framed gate pavilion under upswept dark-prismarine glazed roofs.

japanese.notes: Shoin-style temple precinct entered through a sanmon gate, a covered roka veranda turning at a right angle past a small genkan threshold room and a paper-screen partition, and finally the main hojo hall hidden at the end of the second leg — pale plaster walls, dark spruce posts and smooth-stone stepping paths.

medieval.notes: Fortified gatehouse plan: the outer portcullis opens into a low cobblestone barbican that turns 90 degrees past an inner murder-hole chamber before debouching into the great hall — heavy stone-brick walls, dark-oak rafters and a single chicane that breaks the line of fire from outside the curtain.
